paragraphs.body: It was the dead of winter in Georgia, and as a bored eight-year-old I took it upon myself to rummage through the one of the junk drawers that resided in the kitchen in the hopes that somehow I would pull out a treasure of some sort. I was sitting on the tile floor removing serpentine cords and chargers that crisscrossed into each other like spaghetti. They each belonged to a device that had been lost in the archives of time. I carelessly threw the small black noodles out on the floor and their little USB heads clacked softly against the ceramic. And with further digging found my gaze was drawn to a pearlescent metal blue rectangular object with a mechanical eye in the back right corner of the cluttered drawer. Without hesitation, I grabbed it and as the natural light from the kitchen windows victoriously combated the shadows, I could see that it was a camera. An excitement that I had never felt before in my entire life had rushed into my soul, wiping the dust off its surface I read the Canon brand logo that was printed in white on the left-hand side of the lens. In more specific terms this was the Canon PowerShot A4000. A very basic pocket-sized camera, bit smaller in size than a modern I phone. After rotating the shiny blue and silver object I found a battery slot. But there were no batteries, so I used the oldest trick in the book and seeked my dear Fathers attention by yelling “Daaaaad” with a crescendo in volume that begins after the D, it worked. He helped me find the battery charger and card and after what seemed like an eternity I was able to turn the camera on. Something clicked for me just then, like I cracked the code and unlocked a part of myself that I didn’t even know was there.

annotations.comment: interesting phrase, here, considering the central question of this essay :)

paragraphs.body: A mechanical creature with one eye whose deep black reflective pupil can enlarge and zoom into an object, this eye is made out of multi-coated glass optics. It has evolved over time becoming smaller and more portable while also becoming more technologically advanced. Containing a hard plastic and metal exterior with numerous buttons and dials that ratchet and make clicking sounds, it primarily feasts on batteries and memory cards of different shapes and sizes depending on the species. While typically dawning a black coloration the camera comes in a variety of colors, an adaptation used to attract the attention of humankind. They live in a mutualistic relationship with humans and rely on them for their energy source in exchange for giving humans access to capture life, communicate, and tell stories using visual information including videos and photos. These bits of visual information can be played back and re-shot through a small rectangular screen on the camera called a viewfinder. Film cameras are the predecessor to the aforementioned digital camera and rather than using memory cards they eat thin strips of plastic that are light sensitive and a bit more limited to find out in the wild, these types of cameras view the world in a grainier and more analog format. Although this more retro style has become more popular lately as our human society has been searching for that root feeling of nostalgia. When they are not shooting footage cameras are typically known to be hibernating within soft bags or hard-shell cases lined with foam protection on the interior.

annotations.text: an adaptation used to attract the attention of humankind.

annotations.comment: It's fascinating the way you humanize the camera throughout this paragraph (highlighted in blue...there are probably other places, too).  I look forward to seeing why. Perhaps there's something about how you see the camera as part of your own humanity, as an "alive" thing, an object that creates a very real relationship? Because it keeps other things alive? We'll see :)

paragraphs.body: I remember sitting in the back seat of my Grammies van as she drove my mom and I through her hometown in Ellwood City. I darted my eyes to each dilapidated house that passed the window. At five years old I was on cloud 9 just being there existing with the people I love. But in a flash of lightning I was struck down when I overheard “he passed.” I gathered enough information to understand that Grammie was talking to my mom about a boy dog but had no clue why she sounded so sad. Not understanding I asked, “like passed the street?” as if in slow motion she solemnly replied “No, he died, He’s gone.” All the color had now drained from the world, and I had been reduced to this indescribable, terrifying, and traumatizing feeling. My sheltered little life up until this point seemed full of joy like nothing could happen to anyone or anything. “He died” “died” “he died” is all I remember from the rest of that trip. The memory lives in my head rent free, a still image in black in white of a chain link fence. The fence I was looking at when my Grammie said that word. That day I had realized my greatest fear.

annotations.text: At five years old I was on cloud 9 just being there existing with the people I love.
annotations.context_right: But in a flash of l

annotations.text: head rent free, a still image in black in white of a chain link fence.

paragraphs.body: According to the Mariam Webster dictionary and etymonline.com, the word “died” originates from the 12th century middle English word dien. Which holds the same definition, that being to have passed from physical life. Primarily the word died is often shrouded in gloomy light and generally has negative connotation. For most people, including me, the word has a lot of emotional weight because the second we hear or see it we associate it with the end of organic life. The finality of existence strikes a feeling of grief, sadness, uncertainty, and fear. It is worth acknowledging that the word died also can be framed in a positive connotation. In this positive usage the word can be applied with the release or loss of something that has negative connotation. For example “After talking with the therapist, his worries died.” In my experience I also overhear the word being used in discussions, i.e. “Girl no, that relationship died years ago.” Or even “I just died when I saw that dress oh my god it was so pretty.” So while typically seen as negative the word does have instances of positive meaning and I think in understanding this I can work at shifting my emotions toward it. Looking back on the word died in its context of the end of life, it is remarkable the number of euphemisms there are connected to the word and its other tenses. The following is a detailed list of top slang phrases from mariecurie.org.uk, the following phrases are the ones I personally found most iconic or interesting.

annotations.text: “Girl no, that relationship died years ago.” Or even “I just died when I saw that dress oh my god it was so pretty.”

annotations.comment: I like this thorough exploration of your word, here. It's interesting.
annotations.context_left: being to have passed from physical life. Primarily the word died is often shrouded in gloomy

paragraphs.body: • Kick the Bucket • Gone to a better place • Pushing up Daises • Snuffed it • 6 feet under • Gone to heaven • Popped your/ their/ his/ her/ clogs My phone camera is undoubtedly the one I use the most. When I scroll through all 30,768 videos and photos I find that some of my most valuable footage revolve around death. I have so much footage of Grammie on my camera roll, this is because I was preserving my memories of her enthusiastic and whimsical personality. She had songs to sing that I didn’t want to lose lyrics to, and these hilarious one liners she’d combat you with if she thought you looked at her funny. So I took a video nearly every time she spoke, she had the most endearing smile, so I took her picture whenever I saw her. But she died, she’s gone. I miss her so much.

annotations.text: She had songs to sing that I didn’t want to lose lyrics to, and these hilarious one liners she’d combat you with if she thought you looked at her funny. So I took a video nearly every time she spoke, she had the most endearing smile, so I took her picture whenever I saw her. But she died, she’s gone. I miss her so much.

annotations.comment: I wonder if you need a bit of explaining or linking ideas to create a connection between the close focus on the camera (narrative and descriptive) on the first pages and this exploration of death.  How does the feeling of wonder and excitement of the camera relate to your biggest fear?
annotations.context_left: • Popped your/ their/ his/ her/ clogs

annotations.comment: I think, maybe, that the essay might need a little more talk here about _why_ death is your greatest fear.  Perhaps in a definition paragraph? Or more narrative? Both?

paragraphs.body: Despite this grief her funeral hosted more laughter than tears and of course I felt the need to document it through taking video on my phone, so I did. And the product I got reflected that rather than mourning death we can celebrate life. My videos display a dimly lit room where the soft lighting, floral carpet, wall decorations, and candles almost yell at you to feel miserable. But with each phone recording more strangers from different walks of life come together in the camera frame to share stories, chuckle, and embrace. I had no idea Grammie had so many friends, that day there were upwards of 60 people crammed into the small funeral home. It didn’t feel like a funeral anymore and instead it almost felt like a party- at least it did for me. I think that’s how she would have wanted it.

I still have my shiny blue Canon PowerShotA4000, With the addition of an Asahi Pentax K1000. So I can capture new memories in both digital and film. Sometimes I wonder if my childhood self knew what I was getting into by picking up that little camera. I will always be the person to take another photo when there are already five different versions of the same one, I will always be recording things that may seem boring or insignificant. I view my world through a camera lens to preserve as much as my life experiences the lives of others as much as I can because as afraid of death as I am, I know that tomorrow is not guaranteed. But visual imagery lives on, and can carry my memories and stories, as well as the stories of others.

annotations.text: I view my world through a camera lens to preserve as much as my life experiences the lives of others as much as I can because as afraid of death as I am, I know that tomorrow is not guaranteed.
annotations.comment: Aha!! Yes. This is the central focusing idea. So, I comments above about telling your reader _why_ you're afraid of death is a missing piece in this essay :)

Wow. Beautiful descriptions and a strong voice. This essay is nimble and takes risks with range and depth and structure but never loses control or its "shape" and form. It's an exciting example of a draft that allows for discovery :) I think the essay's central focusing idea might be more clear with just a little more content about _why_ death is your greatest fear (see my notes in-text). Could this section also be, perhaps, in a definition paragraph? Or could part of it be in a definition paragraph? Well done!
